title: Python Solutions Architect Assessment
description: Architect-level evaluation focusing on distributed systems, multi-tenant architecture, polyglot persistence, security patterns, and cloud-native Python scaling.

questions.text: When designing a high-concurrency Python backend, when would you choose an Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) over a standard Request-Response model?
options: When the application needs to be simple to debug
When you need to decouple services, handle high-volume asynchronous tasks, and improve system resilience through eventual consistency
When the database is the primary bottleneck
When using a monolithic architecture
questions.explanation: EDA allows for better scalability and fault tolerance by using message brokers (Kafka/RabbitMQ) to decouple the producer from the consumer, allowing services to scale independently.

questions.text: In a Microservices environment, what is the 'Saga Pattern' used for?
options: To manage long-running database migrations
To manage distributed transactions and maintain data consistency across multiple services without using 2PC (Two-Phase Commit)
To speed up API response times
To encrypt data at rest
questions.explanation: Sagas manage consistency via a sequence of local transactions and compensating transactions in case of failure, which is critical for Python services where distributed locking is expensive.

questions.text: What are the architectural implications of the 'Twelve-Factor App' rule regarding 'Statelessness' for Python web clusters?
options: You must store all user sessions in local memory
The application must share nothing; any persistent data must be stored in a stateful backing service (Redis, Postgres, etc.) to allow for seamless horizontal scaling
Python is naturally stateless, so no action is required
It prevents the use of asynchronous programming
questions.explanation: Statelessness ensures that any instance can handle any incoming request, which is the foundation of containerized orchestration like Kubernetes.

questions.text: Which pattern would you implement to prevent a slow downstream service from causing a cascading failure in your Python API?
options: Retry Pattern
Circuit Breaker Pattern
Singleton Pattern
Proxy Pattern
questions.explanation: Circuit breakers (like Hystrix or custom middleware) 'trip' when a service fails repeatedly, returning an immediate error to the user and saving upstream resources.

questions.text: When should an architect recommend 'BFF' (Backend for Frontend) over a single 'One-Size-Fits-All' API?
options: To reduce the number of microservices
When different clients (Mobile, Web, IoT) require significantly different data shapes, security protocols, or performance optimizations
To simplify the database schema
When the team size is small
questions.explanation: BFF allows you to tailor the API response to the specific needs of a UI, reducing over-fetching and improving mobile performance.

questions.text: What is 'Polyglot Persistence' and why is it common in Python-based architectures?
options: Using multiple programming languages for one database
Using different database technologies (e.g., Postgres for relational data, Redis for caching, Neo4j for graphs) within the same system based on data needs
Storing data in multiple languages (English, Spanish, etc.)
Backing up data in multiple regions
questions.explanation: Python's rich ecosystem of drivers makes it easy to integrate various specialized databases to solve specific storage problems efficiently.

questions.text: How does 'Database Sharding' differ from 'Read Replicas'?
options: Sharding is for reads; Replicas are for writes
Read Replicas copy the whole database to distribute read load; Sharding splits data horizontally across multiple servers to distribute both read and write load
Sharding is a security feature
Replicas are only for NoSQL databases
questions.explanation: Sharding is a scaling strategy for when a single master node can no longer handle the write volume or total data size.

questions.text: What is 'CQRS' (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) and when is it applicable?
options: A way to sort code folders
An architectural pattern that separates the models for reading data from the models for updating data, often used in high-traffic or complex domain systems
A type of encryption
A deployment strategy
questions.explanation: CQRS allows for independent scaling of read and write workloads, often paired with Event Sourcing.

questions.text: In a multi-tenant Python SaaS application, what is the 'Silo' vs 'Pool' isolation model?
options: Silo is for storage; Pool is for compute
Silo gives each tenant their own infrastructure/database; Pool shares infrastructure across all tenants using logical separation (e.g., Tenant_ID columns)
Silo is faster; Pool is more secure
They are the same thing
questions.explanation: Silo provides maximum isolation (compliance), while Pool provides maximum cost-efficiency and ease of management.

questions.text: What is 'API Rate Limiting' (Throttling) and what are the standard algorithms used?
options: Limiting the speed of the internet; FIFO
Limiting the number of requests a user can make; Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, and Fixed Window
Deleting old API keys; Random selection
Limiting the size of JSON payloads; Binary search
questions.explanation: Throttling protects your backend from 'Noisy Neighbors' and DDoS attacks. Token Bucket is widely used for its flexibility with 'bursts'.

questions.text: What is 'Zero Trust Architecture' in a Python backend context?
options: A system that doesn't trust the developers
A security model that requires strict identity verification for every person and device trying to access resources, regardless of whether they are inside or outside the network perimeter
A system without passwords
An air-gapped server
questions.explanation: Zero Trust assumes the network is compromised and enforces granular 'Least Privilege' access at every service hop.

questions.text: How does 'GRPC' compare to 'REST' for internal microservices communication?
options: REST is faster because it uses JSON
GRPC uses Protocol Buffers (binary) and HTTP/2, offering lower latency, smaller payloads, and bi-directional streaming compared to REST/JSON
GRPC is only for frontend-to-backend calls
REST is more secure
questions.explanation: Architects favor gRPC for internal 'service-to-service' talk to reduce network overhead and enforce strongly-typed contracts.

questions.text: What is 'Blue-Green Deployment' and how does it mitigate risk?
options: A way to color-code servers
Running two identical production environments; you switch traffic from the old (Blue) to the new (Green) only after Green is fully tested, allowing for instant rollback
Deploying half of the code on Monday and half on Friday
Using two different cloud providers
questions.explanation: It eliminates downtime during deployment and provides a 'safety net' if the new version contains critical bugs.

questions.text: In Python, why might an architect choose 'Multiprocessing' over 'Threading' for a CPU-intensive task despite the higher memory overhead?
options: Threading is deprecated in Python 3
To bypass the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) and achieve true parallelism on multi-core systems
Multiprocessing is easier to write
Threading only works on Windows
questions.explanation: Because each process has its own Python interpreter and memory space, they don't compete for the GIL, enabling parallel CPU work.

questions.text: What is the 'Strangler Fig Pattern'?
options: A way to delete code
A migration strategy to replace a legacy system by gradually creating new services and 'strangling' the old functionality over time
A data compression algorithm
A type of cyber attack
questions.explanation: This is the preferred way to migrate a Monolith to Microservices without a 'Big Bang' rewrite.

questions.text: What is 'Log Aggregation' (e.g., ELK Stack) and why is it essential for distributed architectures?
options: Storing logs on each server locally
Collecting logs from all services into a central searchable dashboard to enable cross-service troubleshooting and monitoring
Deleting logs to save space
Encrypting logs
questions.explanation: Tracing a single transaction across 5 microservices is impossible without a centralized logging and tracing system (like OpenTelemetry).

questions.text: What is 'Edge Computing' in the context of a Python-based IoT architecture?
options: Putting servers on the edge of a table
Processing data closer to where it is generated (e.g., on the device or a local gateway) to reduce latency and bandwidth usage
Using a specific web browser
The final stage of the development lifecycle
questions.explanation: By running Python scripts on edge nodes, you can filter and process data before sending only the important bits to the cloud.

questions.text: How do 'JWT' (JSON Web Tokens) handle 'Revocation' or 'Logouts' in a stateless architecture?
options: They can't be revoked; you just wait for them to expire
By using short-lived tokens and refresh tokens, or maintaining a 'Denylist' in a fast store like Redis
By deleting the token from the user's computer
By changing the server password
questions.explanation: Revocation is the biggest challenge of stateless JWTs. A Denylist allows you to invalidate a token before its expiry date.

questions.text: What is 'Semantic Versioning' (SemVer) and why is it important for an architect to enforce it?
options: Naming versions after colors
A system (Major.Minor.Patch) that communicates the level of change in an API or library to prevent breaking downstream dependencies
A way to count lines of code
A documentation standard
questions.explanation: Enforcing SemVer ensures that users know when an update is safe (Patch) vs. when it requires code changes (Major).

questions.text: What is 'Infrastructure as Code' (IaC) and which tools are commonly used with Python environments?
options: Writing code on a server
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files (e.g., Terraform, Pulumi, Ansible) rather than manual configuration
Using a specific type of hard drive
Building a data center
questions.explanation: IaC allows you to treat your infrastructure exactly like your code: versioned, tested, and reproducible.

questions.text: What is the 'Clean Architecture' (Hexagonal/Onion) and its primary goal?
options: Making the code look pretty
To separate the business logic from external concerns (DB, UI, Frameworks), making the core application testable and independent of technical details
Using only standard libraries
Writing code without comments
questions.explanation: Clean architecture ensures that you can swap your database or web framework without touching your core business rules.

questions.text: What is 'Data Sovereignty' and how does it affect cloud-based Python architectures?
options: The power of the data
The legal requirement that data is subject to the laws of the country in which it is located (e.g., GDPR), requiring regional data isolation
A type of data ownership
A way to encrypt data
questions.explanation: Architects must design 'Multi-Region' setups to ensure data for German users stays in Germany, etc.

questions.text: What is 'Graceful Degradation'?
options: The server slowly getting older
A design philosophy where a system maintains limited functionality even when a portion of it fails, rather than crashing completely
The code becoming harder to read
A slow network connection
questions.explanation: Example: If the recommendation engine is down, the website still shows the main products instead of a 500 error.

questions.text: What is 'Service Level Objective' (SLO) vs 'Service Level Agreement' (SLA)?
options: SLO is a goal; SLA is a legal contract with consequences for failing to meet the SLO
They are the same
SLA is for internal use; SLO is for customers
SLO is for speed; SLA is for uptime
questions.explanation: Architects design systems to meet the SLO (e.g., 99.9% uptime) to satisfy the SLA.

questions.text: What is 'Optimistic Locking' and when is it preferred over 'Pessimistic Locking'?
options: Thinking the lock will work; always preferred
A strategy where you check if a record has changed before saving (using a version column); preferred in low-contention environments to avoid long-held DB locks
Locking the database for 10 minutes
A security feature
questions.explanation: Optimistic locking improves performance by avoiding the overhead of row-level database locks when conflicts are rare.

questions.text: What is 'Cache Invalidation' and why is it considered a 'Hard Problem'?
options: Deleting the cache; it takes too long
Ensuring the cache is consistent with the source data; it is difficult to determine exactly when data becomes stale across distributed nodes
Encrypting the cache
Setting the cache size
questions.explanation: Stale data can lead to business errors. Choosing between 'Write-Through', 'Write-Back', and 'TTL' is a key architect decision.

questions.text: What is 'Mutation Testing'?
options: Testing for viruses
A type of software testing where small changes (mutations) are introduced to the source code to see if the existing unit tests catch them
Testing code on different OS
Testing how fast code runs
questions.explanation: It measures the 'quality' of your tests, not just the coverage. If a mutant lives, your tests are weak.

questions.text: What is 'Content Delivery Network' (CDN) and what is 'Cache Hit Ratio'?
options: A network for videos; how many people watch them
A distributed network of servers that serves content from the nearest location to the user; the percentage of requests served by the cache vs. the origin
A way to send emails; how many are opened
A backup system
questions.explanation: A high Cache Hit Ratio means your CDN is effectively offloading traffic from your main Python application.

questions.text: What is 'Shadow IT' and how can an architect mitigate it?
options: Using IT at night
The use of systems or software without explicit organizational approval; mitigated by providing flexible, well-documented, and easy-to-use internal platforms
Hacking
Using dark mode in the IDE
questions.explanation: Architects must ensure the approved tools are better/easier than the ones developers might secretly use (e.g., unauthorized SaaS).

questions.text: What is 'Serverless' (FaaS) and what are its 'No-Ops' limitations for Python developers?
options: A server that doesn't work; it can't run Python
An execution model where the cloud provider manages the server; limitations include execution time limits, cold starts, and restricted local file access
Building your own server
Using Python without a computer
questions.explanation: Architects must weigh the cost savings of serverless against the architectural constraints it imposes on long-running or memory-heavy Python tasks.
